payload.summary: Reviewed both amendments against the actual code. Amendment fr-7-6ba24ae1 proposes adding a clarifying invariant comment at json_fill_none call sites. The existing comments at lines 2321-2325 and 2365-2366 say "adding a new optional field automatically includes it in the merge" which is true but subtly misleading — it implies the merge is fully self-maintaining, when in fact fields with conflict semantics require explicit option_conflicts() checks above. A targeted warning is cheap and prevents a real class of silent-data-loss bugs. Amendment fr-7-0cb31894 claims plan_hash backfill is incomplete for existing rows during completion, but the code at lines 3260-3264 contains an explicit NOTE explaining this is intentional: snapshot plan_hash may have advanced after the bead started, so backfilling from it would assign incorrect lineage. The design correctly refuses to guess which plan the bead ran under.

votes.rationale: The existing comments at lines 2321-2325 say 'adding a new optional field automatically includes it in the merge without manual enumeration, so future fields cannot be silently dropped during journal repair.' This is accurate for fill-only semantics but actively misleading for the conflict-detection concern. A developer adding a new optional field (e.g., a hypothetical 'signer_id') that has conflict semantics could read that comment, conclude the merge is self-maintaining, and skip adding an option_conflicts() check — resulting in silently preserving stale base values instead of flagging conflicts. Adding a targeted invariant comment ('any new optional field with conflict semantics MUST have an explicit option_conflicts() check above') is zero-risk, zero-cost, and directly prevents a subtle class of silent-data-loss bugs. The amendment is well-scoped and actionable.

The code at lines 3260-3264 contains an explicit intentional-design NOTE: 'snapshot-based plan_hash backfill is intentionally NOT applied when finalizing an existing row. persist_plan() can advance snapshot.plan_hash after the attempt started, so the current snapshot hash may not match the plan the bead ran under. Only new entry creation auto-populates.' This is a correct design decision. When a bead starts running under plan-v1 but the plan is later persisted as plan-v2, the snapshot reflects plan-v2 — backfilling from it during completion would assign the wrong lineage. The reproduction scenario described (pre-seeding plan_hash=None, then completing with plan_hash=None while snapshot has a value) is precisely the case the NOTE addresses: neither the start record nor the caller provided a plan_hash, so the system correctly refuses to guess from a potentially-stale snapshot. The caller (service.rs:557-563) forwarding plan_hash as-is is also correct — the caller is the authority on which plan version the bead ran under. Auto-populating at start time (line 2957-2961) is safe because the snapshot reflects the plan at the moment the bead begins; auto-populating at completion time is not safe because the snapshot may have changed.
